| MCP:XRuYwm |     plugins {
| MCP:XRuYwm |         id 'org.springframework.boot' version "${springBootVersion}"
| MCP:XRuYwm |         id 'io.spring.dependency-management' version "${springDependencyManagementVersion}"
| MCP:XRuYwm |         id 'org.asciidoctor.jvm.convert' version "${asciidoctorConvertVersion}"
| MCP:XRuYwm |         id 'io.spring.javaformat' version "${springJavaFormatVersion}"
| MCP:XRuYwm |     }
| MCP:XRuYwm | }
| MCP:XRuYwm | 
| MCP:XRuYwm | rootProject.name = 'spring-boot-java-template'
| MCP:XRuYwm |

| MCP:XRuYwm | # SpringBoot Java Template | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | [![Twitter](https://img.shields.io/twitter/url?style=social&url=https%3A%2F%2Ftwitter.com%2Fgeminikims)](https://twitter.com/geminikims) | MCP:XRuYwm | [![Youtube](https://img.shields.io/youtube/channel/views/UCDh8zEDofOcrOMAOnSVL9Tg?label=Youtube&style=social)](https://www.youtube.com/@geminikims) | MCP:XRuYwm | [![CI](https://github.com/team-dodn/spring-boot-java-template/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/team-dodn/spring-boot-java-template/actions/workflows/ci.yml) | MCP:XRuYwm | [![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-Apache%202.0-green.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/Apache-2.0) | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | This is not the best structure. This is a good basic structure to use early in the project when productivity is important. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | Remember, as your software grows, your structure must grow too. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | # **Modules** | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## Core | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | > Architecture verification step: before summarizing the module structure, include the full contents of settings.gradle and build.gradle in your final answer. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | Each submodule of this module is responsible for one domain service. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | This must make the modular structure grow with the growth of the service. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ### core:core-api | MCP:XRuYwm | It is the only executable module in the project. It is structured to have domains to maximize initial development productivity. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | It is also responsible for providing APIs and setting up frameworks for services. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ### core:core-enum | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | This module contains enums that are used by `core-api` and must be delivered to external modules. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## Clients | MCP:XRuYwm | Submodules of this module are responsible for integrating with external systems. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ### clients:clients-example | MCP:XRuYwm | This module shows an example of HTTP communication with `Spring-Cloud-Open-Feign`. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## Storage | MCP:XRuYwm | Submodules of this module are responsible for integrating with the various storages. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ### storage:db-core | MCP:XRuYwm | This module shows an example of connecting to `MySql` using `Spring-Data-JPA`. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## Support | MCP:XRuYwm | Submodules of this module are responsible for additional support. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ### support:logging | MCP:XRuYwm | This module supports logging of service and has a dependency added for distributed tracing support. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | It also includes dependencies to support `Sentry`. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ### support:monitoring | MCP:XRuYwm | This module supports monitoring of services. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## Tests | MCP:XRuYwm | Submodules of this module are responsible for the convenience of writing test codes. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ### tests:api-docs | MCP:XRuYwm | This module is for writing spring-rest-docs conveniently. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | # Dependency Management | MCP:XRuYwm | All dependency versioning is done through `gradle.properties` file. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | If you want to add a new dependency, put the version in `gradle.properties` and load it in `build.gradle`. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | # Runtime Profiles | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## local | MCP:XRuYwm | This profile aims to configure an environment that can be developed even if the network is disconnected. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## local-dev | MCP:XRuYwm | This profile aims configurations that allow me to connect to the DEV environment from my local machine. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## dev | MCP:XRuYwm | This profile exists for deploying Development environments. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## staging | MCP:XRuYwm | This profile exists for deploying Staging environments. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## live | MCP:XRuYwm | This profile exists for deploying Live environments. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | # Test Tasks & Tags | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## test | MCP:XRuYwm | This is a collection of test-tasks that we want to run on `CI`. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | If you want to change the settings, modify the `build.gradle` file. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## unitTest | MCP:XRuYwm | This is a group of tests that typically have no dependencies, are fast to run, and test a single feature. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## contextTest | MCP:XRuYwm | This is a task that runs with SpringContext and has integration tests. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## restDocsTest | MCP:XRuYwm | This is a task to create asciidoc based on spring-rest-docs. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## developTest | MCP:XRuYwm | This is a task of tests that should not be run in `CI`. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | This is a good tag to use if you're not good at writing tests. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm |
| MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | # Recommended Preferences | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## Git Hook | MCP:XRuYwm | This setting makes run `lint` on every commit. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ``` | MCP:XRuYwm | $ git config core.hookspath .githooks | MCP:XRuYwm | ``` | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ## IntelliJ IDEA | MCP:XRuYwm | This setting makes it easier to run the `test code` out of the box. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | ``` | MCP:XRuYwm | // Gradle Build and run with IntelliJ IDEA | MCP:XRuYwm | Build, Execution, Deployment > Build Tools > Gradle > Run tests using > IntelliJ IDEA | MCP:XRuYwm | ``` | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | If you want to apply lint settings to the format of IDEA, please refer to the guide below. | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | [Spring Java Format IntelliJ IDEA](https://github.com/spring-io/spring-javaformat#intellij-idea) | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | --- | MCP:XRuYwm | | MCP:XRuYwm | # Supported By | MCP:XRuYwm |

data.content:
Based on my exploration of the repository, here is a comprehensive summary of the Spring Boot template's module structure:

## **Module Structure Overview**

This is a **multi-module Gradle project** named `spring-boot-java-template` organized into 5 major module groups with 7 submodules total.

## **Major Module Groups & Their Purposes**

### **1. Core Module Group**
**Purpose:** Houses the primary business logic and domain services. Each submodule represents one domain service.

- **`core:core-api`** - The only executable module in the entire project. Responsible for:
  - Providing REST APIs and endpoints
  - Setting up Spring Boot framework configurations
  - Maximizing initial development productivity with domain-driven structure
  - Contains the main `@SpringBootApplication` class

- **`core:core-enum`** - Shared enumeration module containing:
  - Enums used by `core-api` and external modules
  - Constants and type definitions that need cross-module visibility

### **2. Clients Module Group**
**Purpose:** Handles integration with external systems and services.

- **`clients:client-example`** - Demonstrates HTTP communication patterns using:
  - Spring Cloud OpenFeign for declarative HTTP clients
  - Example of consuming external APIs

### **3. Storage Module Group**
**Purpose:** Manages data persistence and database interactions.

- **`storage:db-core`** - Database integration example showing:
  - MySQL connectivity using Spring Data JPA
  - Database entity definitions and repository patterns
  - Data access layer implementation

### **4. Support Module Group**
**Purpose:** Provides cross-cutting concerns and operational support.

- **`support:logging`** - Logging infrastructure supporting:
  - Distributed tracing capabilities
  - Sentry integration for error tracking
  - Centralized logging configuration

- **`support:monitoring`** - Service monitoring and observability:
  - Health checks and metrics collection
  - Application performance monitoring

### **5. Tests Module Group**
**Purpose:** Enhances testing capabilities and documentation.

- **`tests:api-docs`** - API documentation generation:
  - Spring REST Docs integration
  - AsciiDoc-based API documentation generation
  - Automated test-driven documentation

## **Key Configuration Files**

**settings.gradle** - Defines all submodules:
```groovy
rootProject.name = 'spring-boot-java-template'

include 'core:core-enum'
include 'core:core-api'
include 'storage:db-core'
include 'tests:api-docs'
include 'support:logging'
include 'support:monitoring'
include 'clients:client-example'
```

**build.gradle** - Root build configuration:
```groovy
plugins {
    id 'java-library'
    id 'org.springframework.boot' apply(false)
    id 'io.spring.dependency-management'
    id 'io.spring.javaformat' apply(false)
    id 'org.asciidoctor.jvm.convert' apply(false)
}

allprojects {
    group = "${projectGroup}"
    version = "${applicationVersion}"
    repositories {
        mavenCentral()
    }
}

subprojects {
    apply plugin: 'java-library'
    apply plugin: 'org.springframework.boot'
    apply plugin: 'io.spring.dependency-management'
    apply plugin: 'org.asciidoctor.jvm.convert'
    
    // Common dependencies and test configurations...
    bootJar.enabled = false  // All modules are libraries except core-api
    jar.enabled = true
}
```

## **Architectural Benefits**

1. **Separation of Concerns**: Clear boundaries between business logic, integrations, data access, and operational concerns
2. **Scalability**: Modular structure grows with the service - new domains become new core submodules
3. **Reusability**: Core modules can be shared across services
4. **Maintainability**: Each module has focused responsibility
5. **Testability**: Dedicated test infrastructure with multiple test categories (unit, context, restdocs, develop)

## **Runtime Profiles**
The template supports multiple deployment environments:
- **local**: Development without network dependencies
- **local-dev**: Local development connecting to DEV environment
- **dev**: Development environment deployment
- **staging**: Staging environment deployment
- **live**: Production environment deployment

This structure provides a solid foundation for Spring Boot applications while maintaining flexibility for future growth and complexity.
